title: Postoperative Care
subtitle: The nurse cares for the patient after surgery — airway and breathing first, monitoring vitals and the surgical site, recognizing hemorrhage and shock, and preventing atelectasis, clots, ileus, and urinary retention.

patient.name: Postoperative Care
patient.admitDate: The nurse provides postoperative care, prioritizing the airway and monitoring for complications.

nursesNote: The nurse provides postoperative care, always prioritizing the airway and breathing first, then circulation. On arrival the nurse assesses airway patency, respirations, oxygen saturation, vital signs, and level of consciousness, then checks the surgical site, dressing, and drains and assesses pain. A rapid, weak pulse with a falling blood pressure and cool, pale skin can signal hemorrhage or shock, and a saturated dressing with bright red blood is reported and reinforced. To prevent complications the nurse promotes coughing, deep breathing, and incentive spirometry (atelectasis and pneumonia), early ambulation and leg exercises with sequential compression devices (blood clots), monitors bowel sounds for return of function (ileus), and ensures the patient voids within about 6-8 hours (urinary retention). Nausea and vomiting after anesthesia are treated while protecting the airway, and pain is managed to support recovery.
providerOrders: Assess airway, breathing, and circulation first on arrival
Monitor vital signs, level of consciousness, and the surgical site
Reinforce the dressing and report bright red bleeding or shock signs
Promote coughing, deep breathing, spirometry, and early ambulation
Ensure the patient voids within 6-8 hours and monitor bowel sounds

steps.title: Recognize Cues
steps.sub: What is true about postoperative care?

opts.text: Airway and breathing are the first postoperative priority
The nurse monitors the surgical site, dressing, and drains
Early ambulation and leg exercises help prevent blood clots
Pain after surgery should be ignored to avoid giving medication
Coughing, deep breathing, and spirometry prevent atelectasis
The patient should void within about 6-8 hours after surgery
A rapid, weak pulse with falling BP can signal hemorrhage or shock
Absent bowel sounds and distension may indicate a paralytic ileus
Bladder distension with no voiding suggests urinary retention
A saturated dressing with bright red blood is a normal finding
Repositioning and ambulation reduce pneumonia and clot risk
Nausea and vomiting can occur after anesthesia and are monitored

steps.title: Prioritize Hypotheses
steps.sub: Order the immediate post-op assessment on arrival

opts.text: Assess airway patency and breathing
Check circulation, vital signs, and oxygen saturation
Assess level of consciousness
Check the surgical site, dressing, and drains
Assess pain and provide comfort measures
Drowsy but arousable in the first hour
Saturated dressing with bright red bleeding
Pain reported at the incision
Urine output under 30 mL/hr
Mild sore throat after intubation
Rapid weak pulse with falling blood pressure
Small amount of serosanguineous drainage
No voiding with bladder distension 8 hours post-op

steps.title: Take Action
steps.inst: Rank the order of actions. #1 = FIRST.

opts.text: Stay with the patient and apply pressure/reinforce the dressing
Assess vital signs and estimate blood loss
Notify the surgeon
Increase IV fluids and apply oxygen as ordered
Continue to monitor closely and document
Assessing the airway first on arrival
Ignoring a saturated dressing as expected
Encouraging coughing, deep breathing, and spirometry
Keeping the patient in bed for days to rest
Monitoring for the patient to void within 6-8 hours
Withholding ordered pain medication despite severe pain
Applying sequential compression devices
Laying a vomiting, drowsy patient flat on the back

reference: Aligned to the NCSBN NCLEX Test Plan client-need category: Reduction of Risk Potential, using the NCSBN Clinical Judgment Measurement Model. Written from standard nursing references. Pending faculty review.
